[2025-01-20] Trump Issues America First Trade Policy Memorandum Directing Review of Biden-Era Tech Policies
On January 20, 2025, President Trump issued several directives, including the America First Trade Policy memorandum. The memorandum instructed federal agencies to review technology policies enacted by the Biden administration.

Specific directives included the Commerce Secretary assessing U.S. intellectual property rights for Chinese persons and recommending reciprocal actions, and reviewing connected vehicle rulemaking for expanded controls. The Treasury Secretary, in consultation with the Commerce Secretary, was directed to review President Biden’s outbound investment executive order for potential modification or rescission and to assess existing national security controls. Agencies are required to submit these policy reviews by April 1, 2025.

Concurrently, President Trump issued a memorandum imposing a regulatory freeze. This directive ordered agencies not to propose or issue new rules until reviewed and approved by a Trump-appointed or designated department head. It also mandated the withdrawal of rules sent to the Office of the Federal Register but not yet published, for review and approval. This memorandum was published in the Federal Register on January 28, 2025 (90 FR 8249).

Additionally, President Trump issued a pardon proclamation for certain federal offenses related to the events at the U.S. Capitol on January 6, 2021. The proclamation allows eligible individuals to apply for a certificate of pardon through the Department of Justice after completing their sentences.
Key facts:
  • AILA Doc. No. 25012910
  • Addressed to heads of executive departments and agencies
  • America First Trade Policy memorandum issued
  • Commerce Secretary directed to assess U.S. IP rights for PRC persons (Section 3(e))
  • Commerce Secretary directed to review ICTS rulemaking on connected vehicles (Section 4(d))
  • Directs federal agencies to review Biden Administration policies
  • Issued January 20, 2025
  • Issued by President Trump
  • January 20, 2025
  • Mandates withdrawal of rules sent to OFR but not yet published
  • Memorandum dated January 20, 2025
  • Offenses related to January 6, 2021 events
  • Pardon proclamation issued
  • Policy reviews due to President by April 1, 2025
  • President Trump
  • Published in 90 FR 8249 on January 28, 2025
  • Requires review and approval by Trump-appointed/designated agency heads after noon on 1/20/25
  • Treasury Secretary directed to review President Biden’s outbound investment executive order (Section 4(e))
  • United States Capitol
Sources: www.insideglobaltech.com, www.aila.org, www.justice.gov (3 sources, verified date)

[2025-04-02] President Trump Hosts 'Liberation Day' Event on Tariffs, Featuring Auto Workers Brian Pannebecker and Chris Vitale
President Donald Trump hosted a "Liberation Day" event in the White House Rose Garden on Wednesday, April 2, 2025, to announce new tariffs and sign an executive order implementing them.

The new policy includes a minimum 10% tariff on all imports, with specific rates such as 34% on China, 20% on the European Union, 46% on Vietnam, 32% on Taiwan, 49% on Cambodia, and 44% on Sri Lanka. The baseline 10% tariff is scheduled to take effect on April 5, with other tariffs commencing April 9.

Trump also signed an order removing a "de minimis" tariff exemption on low-cost products and proposed an "External Revenue Service" to collect tariffs. The event featured auto workers Brian Pannebecker and Chris Vitale, who voiced support for the tariffs.

Following the announcement, made after U.S. stock markets closed, stock index futures declined. New Zealand's Trade Minister confirmed the impending 10% tariff on its exports but stated no plans for retaliatory measures.
Key facts:
  • 10% base tariff on all foreign imports
  • 20 Michigan UAW members attended
  • 20% tariff on European Union
  • 20-50% tariffs for countries with major tariffs on US goods
  • 32% tariff on Taiwan

  • 46% tariff on Vietnam
  • Attendees: Donald Trump, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, House Speaker Mike Johnson, Vice President JD Vance, Secretary of State Marco Rubio, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick
  • Baseline 10% tariff takes effect April 5, 2025
  • Brian Pannebecker: Founder of Auto Workers for Trump 2024, New Baltimore resident, Sterling Heights native, retired auto worker
  • Cambodia tariff rate: 49 percent
  • Chris Vitale was elected to St. Clair Shores City Council in November 2011 and re-elected in November 2023
  • Chris Vitale: St. Clair Shores city councilman, former Stellantis employee, UAW member
  • Damien O’Connor (Labour trade spokesperson)
  • Estimated $900 million penalty for NZ exporters

"We support Donald Trump’s policies on tariffs 100%." — Brian Pannebecker (Speech at the White House 'Liberation Day' event)
"He’s been a fan of ours and he understands this business a lot better than the economists, a lot better than anybody." — Donald Trump (Introducing Brian Pannebecker at the White House 'Liberation Day' event)
"My entire life I have watched plant after plant after plant in Detroit and the Metro Detroit area close. There are now plants sitting idle, there are now plants under-utilized. Donald Trump’s policies are going to bring product back into these under-utilized plants. There’s going to be new investment, new plants built." — Brian Pannebecker (Speech at the White House 'Liberation Day' event)
"It’s a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity." — Chris Vitale (Before attending the White House 'Liberation Day' event)
"What Trump is doing is fundamentally the right move." — Chris Vitale (Regarding tariffs, before attending the White House 'Liberation Day' event)
"It's our declaration of independence" — Donald Trump (Event in the White House Rose Garden)
"My best speculation here is that is probably a combination of the GST rate plus we do have some tariffs against the US in some products set at a minimum WTO agreed level." — Kelly Eckhold (Commenting on the US claim of a 20% NZ tariff)
"important to note many other countries around the world are facing much higher tariffs" — Todd McClay (Commenting on the impact of tariffs on New Zealand)
"Whilst not unexpected, it will have an impact on New Zealand exports." — Todd McClay (Commenting on the impact of tariffs on New Zealand)
"put up prices on New Zealand consumers and be inflationary" — Todd McClay (Explaining why New Zealand will not retaliate against US tariffs)
"The government’s strategy of keeping its head down has not given New Zealand any advantage over our competitors." — Damien O’Connor (Criticizing the New Zealand government's approach to tariffs)
"It’s disappointing that the government hasn’t been able to negotiate lower tariffs given the very low level of tariffs we impose on goods and services from the US." — Damien O’Connor (Criticizing the New Zealand government's approach to tariffs)
"There’s going to be a $900 million hit on our exports, and there is uncertainty over who will carry the cost of that, whether it will be US consumers or New Zealand exporters." — Damien O’Connor (Commenting on the financial impact of the tariffs)
"It's going to be a day that hopefully you're going to look back in years to come and you're going to say, 'You know, he was right. This has turned out to be one of the most important days in the history of our country,'" — Donald Trump (Remarks at 'Liberation Day' event announcing tariffs)
"I am today announcing that I will create the EXTERNAL REVENUE SERVICE to collect our Tariffs, Duties, and all Revenue thatcome from Foreign sources," — Donald Trump (Social media post on January 14, prior to inauguration)
"My fellow Americans, this is Liberation Day." — Donald Trump (Rose Garden event announcing tariffs)
"April 2, 2025, will forever be remembered as the day American industry was reborn, the day America's destiny was reclaimed, and the day that we began to make American wealthy again." — Donald Trump (Rose Garden event announcing tariffs)
"Well, the stock market's closed, but the futures are tanking." — Amy Klobuchar (Senate floor following Trump's proclamation)
"The President is risking a recession with this national sales tax he calls tariffs—while needlessly alienating our closest allies." — Dick Durbin (Statement regarding the tariffs)
"President Trump seems enamored with tariffs, but he's going to have a heck of a time explaining how this is going to help reduce the cost of living for Americans." — Dick Durbin (Statement regarding the tariffs)
"American families need relief from rising prices and stagnant growth, but instead, Donald Trump—with buy-in from Congressional Republicans—is administering economic poison to our economy." — Ron Wyden (Statement regarding the tariffs)
"Nevada families are already suffering from high costs on everything from housing, to groceries, to gas. Trump's sweeping tariffs are a slap in the face to hardworking Nevadans who will now have to bear the full brunt of these additional taxes." — Jackie Rosen (Statement regarding the tariffs)
"President Trump is sending a clear message with Liberation Day: America will not be exploited by unfair trade practices anymore." — Mike Johnson (X post)
"These tariffs restore fair and reciprocal trade and level the playing field for American workers and innovators." — Mike Johnson (X post)
"The President understands that free trade only works when it's fair!" — Mike Johnson (X post)
"Whatever tariffs they impose on us, we will impose on them." — Rep. Tom Tiffany (X post)
"President Trump's reciprocal tariffs will bring back fair trade and put American workers first." — Rep. Tom Tiffany (X post)
"Thanks to President Trump, foreign nations can no longer take advantage of America's generosity." — Rep. Andy Biggs (X post)
"President Trump is bringing back the American Dream." — Rep. Diana Harshbarger (Statement regarding the tariffs)
"Our taxpayers have been ripped off by foreign countries for far too long, but those days are over. President Trump is right to impose these reciprocal tariffs." — Rep. Diana Harshbarger (Statement regarding the tariffs)
"strengthen American manufacturing and create millions of American jobs." — Rep. Elise Stefanik (Statement regarding Trump's new policies)
"For too long, Americans have suffered under unfair trade practices putting America Last." — Rep. Elise Stefanik (Statement regarding Trump's new policies)
"We will not allow other countries to take advantage of us and we must put America and the American worker first." — Rep. Elise Stefanik (Statement regarding Trump's new policies)

[2025-02-10] President Trump Orders Reinstatement of 25% Tariffs on Steel and Steel Derivatives, Increases Aluminum Tariffs to 25%, and Terminates Country Exemptions
President Donald J. Trump signed proclamations on February 10, 2025, reinstating 25% tariffs on all steel imports and increasing tariffs on aluminum imports from 10% to 25%. The actions, taken under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, terminated prior alternative agreements that had provided tariff relief to countries including Argentina, Australia, Brazil, Canada, the European Union, Japan, Mexico, South Korea, Ukraine, and the United Kingdom. These countries will now be subject to the full 25% tariff.

Effective March 12, 2025, all steel, steel derivative products, and aluminum products previously covered by Section 232 will be subject to a 25% tariff. The proclamations also imposed an additional 25% ad valorem duty on certain new derivative steel and aluminum articles. The Commerce Secretary's authority to grant new product exclusion requests or renew existing ones was eliminated, effective immediately. A narrow exclusion was created for derivative steel and aluminum products originating from U.S.-melted or smelted articles. By May 11, 2025, the Commerce Secretary must establish a process for U.S. producers to request the inclusion of additional derivative products under these tariffs.
Key facts:

[2025-04-02] President Trump Declares National Emergency Regarding Trade Deficits and Directs Reciprocal Tariffs
President Donald J. Trump declared a national emergency and signed an Executive Order on April 2, 2025, citing U.S. goods trade deficits. The order identified a lack of reciprocity in bilateral trade relationships, disparate tariff rates, non-tariff barriers, and U.S. trading partners’ economic policies as a threat to national security and the economy. Invoking the International Emergency Economic Powers Act of 1977 (IEEPA), the order directs the imposition of responsive tariffs. A 10% tariff will apply to all countries effective April 5, 2025. Individualized higher tariffs will be imposed on countries with the largest trade deficits with the U.S., effective April 9, 2025. The order states these tariffs will remain until the threat is resolved. Exemptions include specific steel/aluminum articles, autos/auto parts, copper, pharmaceuticals, semiconductors, lumber, bullion, and certain energy/minerals. The action follows investigations directed by presidential memorandums in January and February 2025.
Key facts:

[2025-01-31] President Trump Issues Executive Order 'Unleashing Prosperity Through Deregulation' to Reduce Federal Regulation
President Donald J. Trump issued an executive order titled "Unleashing Prosperity Through Deregulation" on January 31, 2025. The order mandates that federal agencies identify and repeal at least 10 existing rules, regulations, or guidance documents for every new one they issue. For fiscal year 2025, the order requires that the total incremental cost of all new regulations, including repealed ones, must be less than zero. The Director of the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) is tasked with standardizing the measurement and estimation of regulatory costs and implementing the order. Regulations concerning the military, national security, homeland security, foreign affairs, immigration, and agency organization/management/personnel are exempt. The order also revokes the 2023 update to OMB Circular A-4, reinstating the 2003 version, which changes discount rates to 3% and 7% and removes directives to consider effects outside the U.S. and emphasize distributional effects. Starting FY 2026, agencies must report regulatory costs/savings, and the OMB Director will set an incremental cost allowance for each agency.
Key facts:

[2025-04-02] President Trump Signs Executive Order Declaring National Emergency Over Trade Deficits and Implementing Reciprocal Tariffs
On April 2, 2025, President Donald Trump signed Executive Order 14257, titled "Regulating Imports With a Reciprocal Tariff To Rectify Trade Practices That Contribute to Large and Persistent Annual United States Goods Trade Deficits." The order declared a national emergency over the U.S. trade deficit, citing it as an unusual and extraordinary threat to national security and the economy due to factors including a lack of reciprocity and disparate tariff rates. Invoking the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) and the National Emergencies Act, the order authorized tariffs. A baseline 10% tariff for nearly all countries took effect April 5, with higher country-specific rates for major trading partners scheduled for April 9. Trump also signed Executive Order 14256, closing the de minimis exemption for imports from China. The announcement, which Trump designated "Liberation Day," contributed to the 2025 stock market crash. The U.S. Court of International Trade later ruled on May 28, 2025, that Trump had overstepped his authority under IEEPA, ordering the tariffs vacated.
Key facts:
  • 10% baseline tariff
  • 2025 stock market crash
  • April 2, 2025 ('Liberation Day')
  • China de minimis exemption closed

[2025-01-20] President Issues Memorandum Ordering Regulatory Freeze Pending Review for Executive Departments and Agencies
On January 20, 2025, President Trump issued a memorandum titled "Regulatory Freeze Pending Review" to the heads of executive departments and agencies. The directive ordered an immediate halt to proposing or issuing new rules without approval from department or agency heads appointed after January 20, 2025. Agencies were instructed to withdraw any rules sent to the Office of the Federal Register (OFR) but not yet published. The memorandum also directed agencies to consider postponing for 60 days the effective date of rules already published or issued but not yet in effect, to allow for review of factual, legal, and policy questions. The freeze applies to 'rules' as defined by the Administrative Procedure Act and Executive Order 12866. Exceptions are granted for emergency situations or rules subject to statutory or judicial deadlines, with the Director or Acting Director of the Office of Management and Budget authorized to grant such exemptions and oversee implementation. Similar regulatory freezes have been issued by previous administrations.
Key facts:

[2025-03-12] Trump EPA Announces Reconsideration of Air Rules for American Industrial Sectors
U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Administrator Lee Zeldin announced multiple regulatory changes on March 12, 2025. The changes include the reconsideration of National Emission Standards for Hazardous Air Pollutants (NESHAPs) and the repeal of other environmental regulations. The actions affect industrial sectors such as energy, manufacturing, and chemical, as well as limits on vehicle pollution, wetlands protections, and the 2009 EPA "endangerment finding" on greenhouse gas emissions. The EPA stated its aim to ensure appropriate protections for human health and the environment, while also seeking to reduce regulatory burdens, promote domestic energy production, and shift authority to states. The agency is considering a 2-year compliance exemption for affected facilities under the Clean Air Act during the rulemaking process. Zeldin stated the EPA's purpose is to "lower the cost of buying a car, heating a home and running a business."
Key facts:

  • President Donald Trump's executive orders and economic agenda
  • Proposed revision of 2009 EPA "endangerment finding"
  • Regulations include limits on tailpipe and smokestack pollution, wetlands protections, and legal basis for regulating greenhouse gases
  • Repeal of dozens of environmental regulations announced
  • Risk Management Program (RMP) rule
  • Science Advisory Board (SAB)
  • Section 112(i)(4) of the Clean Air Act
  • State Implementation Plans (SIPs)
  • Technology Transition Rule
  • Tribal Implementation Plans (TIPs)
  • Trump administration
  • Video posted to X, 2 minutes 18 seconds in duration
"EPA is moving forward with the reconsideration of a number of air rules that cover nearly every sector of the American economy. During this review, we will ensure the appropriate protections for human health and the environment based on the best record possible. Breathing clean air, living on clean land, and drinking clean water are all shared goals while we also do our part to usher in a Golden Age of American success." — EPA Administrator Zeldin (Announcement of NESHAPs reconsideration)
"biggest deregulatory action in U.S. history" — Lee Zeldin (Describing EPA's deregulatory effort)
"lower the cost of buying a car, heating a home and running a business" — Lee Zeldin (Video posted to X reframing the EPA's mission)
"no obligation to promote agriculture or commerce; only the critical obligation to protect and enhance the environment" — William D. Ruckelshaus (Explaining the EPA's mission weeks after its creation in 1970)
